system: mem0 OSS (Python SDK) — the `Memory` / `AsyncMemory` core in mem0/mem0/memory/main.py. Personal/agent memory layer: LLM fact extraction over chat turns, embedding into a vector store, a parallel vector-based "entity store" for graph-like boosting, hybrid (semantic + BM25 + entity-boost) retrieval, and a SQLite changelog. Default vector store is Qdrant.
architecture_overview:
Single fat module `mem0/memory/main.py` (~3540 lines) holding both sync `Memory` and async `AsyncMemory` as near-duplicate classes (the async half re-implements every method with `asyncio.to_thread` wrappers — ~2x code, drift risk). `Memory.__init__` (main.py:408) wires four pluggable components via factories (mem0/utils/factory.py): embedder, vector_store, llm, optional reranker, plus a `SQLiteManager` (storage.py) for an append-only history/messages log. A second vector collection ("<collection>_entities") is created lazily as `self.entity_store` (main.py:478-500) and acts as the "graph" — there is NO external graph DB in this code path.

The public surface (add/get/get_all/search/update/delete/delete_all/history/reset) is defined abstractly in memory/base.py (a thin ABC). Retrieval/scoring math lives in utils/scoring.py; entity NLP in utils/entity_extraction.py (spaCy); BM25 lemmatization in utils/lemmatization.py. Prompts are in configs/prompts.py. The data flow for add(): parse messages -> fetch last-10 raw messages + top-10 existing memories from the same session -> ONE LLM extraction call -> batch embed -> md5 dedup -> batch insert to vector store -> batch entity-link -> save raw messages + history. search(): lemmatize+entity-extract query -> semantic search (over-fetch 4x) + BM25 keyword_search + entity-store boost -> additive `score_and_rank` -> optional reranker.

key_files.role: Core Memory/AsyncMemory classes: add/search/update/delete pipelines, hybrid search assembly, entity linking. ~3540 lines.

All prompts. ADDITIVE_EXTRACTION_PROMPT (the LIVE ADD-only extractor, lines 468-944) + generate_additive_extraction_prompt builder (1016-1062). DEFAULT_UPDATE_MEMORY_PROMPT (176-324) and get_update_memory_messages (406-460) are DEAD CODE — the legacy 2-phase prompt, no longer wired into add().
Hybrid scoring: BM25 sigmoid normalization, query-length-adaptive params, additive score_and_rank with adaptive divisor. ENTITY_BOOST_WEIGHT=0.5.
spaCy-based entity extraction (PROPER/QUOTED/COMPOUND/NOUN) — rule-heavy, ~360 lines of heuristics. Feeds the entity store and query-time boosting.
spaCy lemmatizer producing text_lemmatized for BM25 indexing and query lemmatization.
SQLiteManager: append-only `history` changelog table + `messages` rolling buffer (last 10/session). Thread-locked, single shared connection.
VectorStoreBase ABC. keyword_search() returns None by default (opt-in BM25); search_batch() default = sequential search().
Default store. Real BM25 via fastembed Qdrant/bm25 sparse vectors (keyword_search, line 418); dense+sparse named vectors. Guards _has_bm25_slot for pre-v3 collections.
Optional 3rd-stage reranker. Per-doc LLM 0-1 relevance score (N sequential LLM calls). Other rerankers: cohere, huggingface, sentence_transformer, zero_entropy.

data_model:
Memory record = one Qdrant point. id = uuid4 string. vector = dense embedding of the extracted fact text. payload (built in _add_to_vector_store, main.py:907-916): {`data`: fact text, `text_lemmatized`: BM25 text, `hash`: md5(text) hex, `created_at`/`updated_at`: ISO8601 UTC, optional `attributed_to` ("user"/"assistant"), plus session scope keys `user_id`/`agent_id`/`run_id` and optional `actor_id`/`role` merged from metadata}. MemoryItem pydantic model (configs/base.py) is the API-facing shape: {id, memory, hash, created_at, updated_at, score}; extra payload keys are promoted or nested under `metadata` on read (get/get_all/_search_vector_store, e.g. main.py:1547-1580).

Entity record = one point in the parallel `<collection>_entities` store (main.py:1047-1052): {`data`: entity text, `entity_type`: PROPER/QUOTED/COMPOUND/NOUN, `linked_memory_ids`: list[str] of memory uuids, + session scope keys}. This list IS the graph adjacency — entity->memories. There are no edges between memories except the LLM-emitted `linked_memory_ids` returned during extraction, which (notably) are NOT persisted anywhere in the OSS write path (see write_path landmine).

SQLite `history` table (storage.py:108-119): (id TEXT PK, memory_id, old_memory, new_memory, event[ADD/UPDATE/DELETE], created_at, updated_at, is_deleted INT, actor_id, role). `messages` table (storage.py:132-141): (id, session_scope, role, content, name, created_at) — rolling last-10 per session_scope, where session_scope = sorted "user_id=..&agent_id=.." string (main.py:358-365).
write_path:
add() (main.py:653) -> _add_to_vector_store() (main.py:761). THIS IS A SINGLE-PASS, ADD-ONLY V3 PIPELINE — not the 2-phase ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP described in the mem0 paper/README. The classic update reconciliation (DEFAULT_UPDATE_MEMORY_PROMPT, get_update_memory_messages in prompts.py) is fully DEAD CODE — grep confirms it is imported/called nowhere.

infer=False branch (main.py:762-796): each message stored verbatim as its own memory via _create_memory(), event=ADD. No extraction, no dedup beyond md5 inside _create_memory.

infer=True phased pipeline:
- Phase 0 (main.py:801-803): build session_scope, pull last 10 raw messages from SQLite (`get_last_messages`), parse new messages.
- Phase 1 (806-820): embed the concatenated new messages, semantic-search the vector store top_k=10 within session filters to get "existing memories." UUIDs are remapped to integer string ids ("0","1",..) with a uuid_mapping — an explicit anti-hallucination measure so the LLM never sees/echoes raw UUIDs.
- Phase 2 (822-862): ONE LLM call. system = ADDITIVE_EXTRACTION_PROMPT (+ AGENT_CONTEXT_SUFFIX if agent-scoped). user prompt built by generate_additive_extraction_prompt with sections: Summary, Last k Messages, Recently Extracted, Existing Memories (the integer-id list), New Messages, Observation Date, Current Date, optional Custom Instructions. Response forced to json_object; parsed with remove_code_blocks + json.loads(strict=False) + extract_json fallback. Extracts `{"memory":[{id,text,attributed_to,linked_memory_ids}]}`.
- Phase 3 (869-881): batch-embed all extracted texts (embed_batch), fallback to per-text embed.
- Phase 4/5 DEDUP (883-921): build `existing_hashes` from md5 of retrieved existing memories; per extracted fact compute `mem_hash = hashlib.md5(text.encode()).hexdigest()` (main.py:898) and skip if in existing_hashes OR seen in this batch (seen_hashes). DEDUP IS EXACT-STRING md5 ONLY — no semantic/near-dup merge at write time. The LLM is asked to skip semantic dups via the prompt, but there is no code enforcement.
- Phase 6 (923-962): batch vector_store.insert (fallback one-by-one), then batch SQLite history inserts (all event=ADD).
- Phase 7 ENTITY LINKING (964-1065): extract_entities_batch(spaCy) over all new texts; global-dedup entities by lower(text); batch-embed unique entities; entity_store.search_batch top_k=1; if best match score >= 0.95 -> merge memory_ids into that entity's linked_memory_ids (update), else collect for a single batch insert of new entity points.
- Phase 8 (1067-1081): save raw messages to SQLite, return [{id,memory,event:"ADD"}].

So every extracted fact is ALWAYS an ADD. Contradiction/supersession is handled only by (a) the extractor being told to skip dups and (b) retrieval-time entity links — never by deleting/updating prior memories. The LLM-returned `linked_memory_ids` are accepted in the schema but never written to storage in this OSS path (the entity store builds its own links independently from spaCy).
read_path:
search() (main.py:1243) -> _search_vector_store() (main.py:1488). Real 3-signal hybrid, then optional rerank:
- Step1-2 (1494-1498): lemmatize query (lemmatize_for_bm25), extract query entities (spaCy), embed query.
- Step3 (1500-1504): semantic search with over-fetch `internal_limit = max(limit*4, 60)` to build a scoring pool.
- Step4-5 (1506-1519): vector_store.keyword_search(lemmatized_query) — returns None on stores without BM25 (base.py:68). For Qdrant it is a real fastembed Qdrant/bm25 sparse-vector query (qdrant.py:418). Raw BM25 scores normalized to [0,1] via logistic sigmoid `normalize_bm25` with query-length-adaptive (midpoint,steepness) from get_bm25_params (scoring.py:16-54): <=3 terms -> (5.0,0.7) scaling up to (12.0,0.5) for >15 terms.
- Step6 (1521-1524): _compute_entity_boosts (main.py:1588) — embed query entities (max 8), ThreadPoolExecutor(4) parallel search of entity store top_k=500, keep matches with sim>=0.5, boost = sim * 0.5 * memory_count_weight where memory_count_weight = 1/(1+0.001*(num_linked-1)^2) (down-weights promiscuous entities). Each linked memory gets max boost.
- Step8 (1536-1544): score_and_rank (scoring.py:60). Threshold (default 0.1) GATES the semantic score BEFORE combining (scoring.py:111) — a memory below threshold semantically can never be rescued by BM25/entity. combined = (semantic + bm25 + entity_boost) / max_possible, where max_possible adapts: 1.0 semantic-only, +1.0 if any BM25 present, +0.5 if any entity boost present (so the divisor is global across the batch, not per-row — rows lacking BM25 are penalized when others have it). Sort desc, take top_k.
- Rerank (main.py:1360-1365): only if rerank=True AND a reranker is configured. LLMReranker does N sequential LLM calls (one per doc) scoring 0-1 (llm_reranker.py:104) — O(N) latency, no batching.

explain=True surfaces per-signal score_details. The reciprocal-rank-fusion / weighted-RRF often described for mem0 is NOT here; fusion is this simple normalized additive sum.
conflict_resolution_impl:
Write-time conflict resolution is effectively ABSENT in the live OSS path. The only write dedup is exact md5 string match (main.py:898-902) against the top-10 semantically-retrieved existing memories and within-batch. There is no UPDATE or DELETE of prior memories during add() — the V3 pipeline only ever emits ADD events (main.py:1070). Contradictions ("I love hiking" then "I hate hiking now") produce TWO coexisting memories; resolution is deferred to (a) the extraction LLM voluntarily not re-extracting per prompt instructions, and (b) retrieval surfacing both and letting the consumer sort it out.

The classic mem0 conflict engine — DEFAULT_UPDATE_MEMORY_PROMPT (prompts.py:176-324) with ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NONE and old_memory tracking — still exists in source but is dead (no caller). Standalone update(memory_id, data) (main.py:1670) and delete() exist for manual/explicit edits and DO maintain history + entity-store cleanup (_update_memory main.py:1839: re-embeds, bumps updated_at, keeps created_at, treats actor_id as immutable, strips+re-links entities), but nothing calls them automatically from add(). Net: this is an accretive memory model, not a self-reconciling one.
forgetting_impl: No automatic forgetting/decay/TTL/consolidation in the OSS code. There are decay/temporal "usage notices" (memory/notices.py) that detect when a user attempts decay/temporal features and raise errors stating these are platform-only / unsupported in OSS (e.g. get_decay_feature_error_message, _OSSProject.update rejects decay=True at main.py:388). The `messages` table self-prunes to the last 10 per session (storage.py:282-291) — that is a context-window cap, not memory forgetting. The `history` table is append-only and never garbage-collected (is_deleted is a tombstone flag set on delete, rows retained). Memories persist until an explicit delete()/delete_all()/reset(). Entity records are GC'd only as a side effect: when a memory is deleted/updated, its id is stripped from entities' linked_memory_ids and the entity row is deleted when its list becomes empty (_remove_memory_from_entity_store, main.py:545-598).

borrowable.pattern: UUID->integer remapping before showing existing memories to the extraction LLM (anti-hallucination)
borrowable.why_good: LLMs fabricate/garble long UUIDs. Mapping retrieved memories to '0','1',.. and translating back in code removes a whole class of hallucinated-id bugs while keeping linking possible.

borrowable.pattern: Single-call phased ADD pipeline with batched embed/insert/history + per-stage fallbacks
borrowable.why_good: One LLM call per add (not one-per-fact), batch embeddings, batch vector insert, batch SQLite history — each wrapped in try/except with one-by-one fallback. Good latency/robustness template even if you adopt 2-phase reconciliation.

borrowable.pattern: Adaptive additive hybrid scoring with semantic threshold gate
borrowable.why_good: Clean, debuggable alternative to RRF: normalize each signal to [0,1], sum, divide by an adaptive max_possible so scores stay in [0,1] regardless of which signals fired. explain=True emits per-signal breakdown — excellent for tuning/observability.

borrowable.pattern: Query-length-adaptive BM25 sigmoid normalization
borrowable.why_good: Raw BM25 is unbounded and grows with query length; mapping to [0,1] with length-tuned (midpoint,steepness) makes it directly combinable with cosine similarity. Cheap and effective.

borrowable.pattern: Vector-store-as-graph entity index with promiscuity down-weighting
borrowable.why_good: Avoids a separate graph DB: entities are points whose payload holds linked_memory_ids. Boost = sim*0.5*1/(1+0.001*(n-1)^2) prevents hub entities (e.g. 'User') from boosting everything. Parallelized with a 4-thread pool.

borrowable.pattern: Session-scoped rolling raw-message buffer in SQLite to give the extractor recent context
borrowable.why_good: Keeping last-10 raw turns per session_scope and feeding them as 'Last k Messages' lets the extractor resolve pronouns/temporal refs without re-sending full history. Self-evicting query is a tidy pattern.

borrowable.pattern: Capability-probe for hybrid search at init with graceful degradation
borrowable.why_good: keyword_search() defaults to None in the base class; init checks whether the subclass overrode it and warns once. Hybrid silently degrades to semantic-only on unsupported stores instead of crashing.

landmines.issue: add() is ADD-ONLY. No automatic UPDATE/DELETE/contradiction resolution. Marketing/paper describe a 2-phase ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP memory manager; that code (DEFAULT_UPDATE_MEMORY_PROMPT, get_update_memory_messages) is present but DEAD. Contradictory facts accumulate as separate rows. If you need self-reconciling memory you must build it.

Write-time dedup is exact md5 string match only. 'User likes pizza' vs 'User loves pizza' are NOT deduped at write. The extractor is merely *told* to skip semantic dups in the prompt; nothing enforces it.

LLM-emitted linked_memory_ids are accepted in the extraction schema but never persisted in the OSS write path. Memory-to-memory links advertised by the prompt/examples don't actually exist as stored edges; the only links are spaCy-entity links built independently.
landmines.file_ref: mem0/mem0/configs/prompts.py:924-936 vs main.py:893-1065 (no write of linked_memory_ids)

landmines.issue: Existing-memory retrieval for extraction is hardcoded top_k=10 and dedup hashes only come from those 10. A duplicate fact outside the top-10 semantic neighbors will be re-added.

Hybrid divisor (max_possible) is computed globally per query, not per row. If ANY candidate has a BM25 score, every candidate's combined score is divided by a larger number, penalizing rows that legitimately had no keyword overlap.

Threshold gates the raw semantic score before fusion, so a memory with a perfect keyword/entity match but mediocre cosine sim is dropped entirely. Tuning threshold interacts non-obviously with hybrid recall.
Sync and Async classes are full copies (~1500 lines each). Any fix must be applied twice; they can and likely will drift.

Hybrid/BM25 requires the right store (Qdrant with fastembed) AND a v3-era collection with the 'bm25' sparse slot. Pre-v3 Qdrant collections silently disable BM25 (_has_bm25_slot=False); FAISS/Chroma have no keyword_search so hybrid is silently semantic-only.

SQLiteManager uses one shared connection guarded by a single threading.Lock with check_same_thread=False. Fine for a library, but serializes all history writes and is not a concurrency story for a multi-worker production service.

Entity extraction is ~360 lines of hand-tuned spaCy POS/dependency heuristics (stopword lists, generic-head sets, compound splitting). Brittle, English-biased, and the silent backbone of both linking and query boosting; failures degrade silently to no-boost.
On LLM extraction failure add() returns [] and silently drops the turn (only logs error); raw messages are saved but no memory is created.

production_readiness: Mixed: production-deployed open-source library, but the memory CORE shown here is mid-migration and uneven. Strengths: extensive input validation (entity-id trimming, threshold/top_k checks, top-level-param rejection), telemetry/secret redaction, capability probing with graceful degradation, batched IO with per-stage fallbacks, transactional SQLite with BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK and a schema-migration path, broad vector-store/embedder/llm provider matrix, and a dedicated test tree (tests/memory, tests/utils/test_scoring.py, test_entity_extraction.py). Weaknesses for building a REAL product on it: (1) the headline 2-phase memory-management algorithm is dead code — you get an accretive ADD-only store, so you must build conflict resolution, semantic dedup, decay/forgetting, and consolidation yourself (none exist in OSS; decay/temporal are explicitly platform-only and raise errors). (2) Massive sync/async duplication invites drift. (3) Memory-to-memory links are advertised but not persisted. (4) Retrieval correctness has real but subtle scoring footguns (global divisor, pre-fusion threshold gate). (5) Entity layer is brittle English-centric spaCy heuristics doing load-bearing work silently. (6) SQLite single-connection+lock is not a multi-worker concurrency story. Overall: a solid, well-instrumented RETRIEVAL and storage substrate (hybrid search, entity boosting, history log) that is genuinely borrowable — but the 'intelligent memory management' is research-grade/aspirational in OSS and would need to be implemented for a true production personal-memory product. Treat main.py as reference for the read path and pipeline ergonomics, not as a turnkey memory brain.
notable_prompts: ADDITIVE_EXTRACTION_PROMPT (configs/prompts.py:468-944): the LIVE system prompt. A very long, example-heavy 'Memory Extractor' whose ONLY operation is ADD. Extracts contextually-rich self-contained facts (15-80 words) from both user and assistant turns, grounds relative time against an Observation Date (not Current Date), preserves proper nouns/numbers, forbids fabrication/echo/meta-extraction, and emits {memory:[{id,text,attributed_to,linked_memory_ids}]}. Deduplication and 'linking' are *instructed* here, not code-enforced.

generate_additive_extraction_prompt (configs/prompts.py:1016-1062): the user-message builder. Assembles Summary, Last k Messages (truncated to 300 chars each), Recently Extracted Memories, Existing Memories (as integer-id JSON), New Messages, Observation Date, Current Date, optional Custom Instructions, optional multilingual clause.
AGENT_CONTEXT_SUFFIX (configs/prompts.py:947-957): appended when the session is agent-scoped (agent_id set, no user_id) to reframe facts from the agent's perspective ('Agent was informed that...').

DEFAULT_UPDATE_MEMORY_PROMPT + get_update_memory_messages (configs/prompts.py:176-460): the CLASSIC 2-phase ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NONE reconciliation prompt with old_memory tracking. DEAD CODE in this build — imported/called nowhere; documents what the live pipeline intentionally dropped.

PROCEDURAL_MEMORY_SYSTEM_PROMPT (configs/prompts.py:326-403): used only by _create_procedural_memory for agent-trajectory summarization (memory_type='procedural_memory'); verbatim action/result step log.
LLMReranker._SYSTEM_PROMPT (reranker/llm_reranker.py:75-86): 0.0-1.0 relevance scoring rubric, one LLM call per candidate doc, used only when rerank=True and an LLM reranker is configured.
storage_impl: Two stores. (1) Vector store (default Qdrant, configs/vector_stores/configs.py:9; pluggable via VectorStoreFactory). Memories are points: dense embedding + payload (data/text_lemmatized/hash/timestamps/scope ids). Qdrant collections also carry a named 'bm25' SPARSE vector (qdrant.py:154-160) encoded by fastembed 'Qdrant/bm25' over text_lemmatized at insert time (qdrant.py:201-206) — this is what makes hybrid real. A SECOND vector collection '<collection>_entities' holds entity points {data, entity_type, linked_memory_ids[], scope ids} and serves as the graph index (main.py:478-500, 1047-1052). (2) SQLite via SQLiteManager (storage.py): append-only `history` changelog (every ADD/UPDATE/DELETE with old/new text, timestamps, is_deleted tombstone, actor_id, role) and a rolling `messages` buffer (last 10 raw turns per session_scope) used to feed recent context into extraction. Single sqlite3 connection, check_same_thread=False, all mutations under one threading.Lock with explicit BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK; includes an in-place schema migration that renames the old table, recreates, copies intersecting columns (storage.py:20-100).

system: Graphiti (Zep) — temporally-aware knowledge graph memory. LLM-driven entity/edge extraction from "episodes" into a bi-temporal property graph (Neo4j/FalkorDB/Kuzu/Neptune), with hybrid retrieval (BM25 + cosine + graph BFS) fused by RRF/MMR/cross-encoder. Apache-2.0, `graphiti_core/` package.
architecture_overview: Episode-centric ingestion pipeline orchestrated in `graphiti.py::add_episode` (graphiti_core/graphiti.py:980-1228). Three node tiers: EpisodicNode (raw input, immutable event), EntityNode (deduped semantic entity), CommunityNode (clustered summary). Relationships are EntityEdge (RELATES_TO, carries the "fact" + bi-temporal stamps), EpisodicEdge (MENTIONS, episode->entity provenance), plus Saga/HAS_EPISODE/NEXT_EPISODE for grouping. Each add_episode runs a fixed multi-stage LLM pipeline: (1) extract entities, (2) resolve/dedupe entities against graph via embedding search + deterministic MinHash + LLM, (3) extract edges, (4) resolve/dedupe + invalidate edges via hybrid search + LLM, (5) extract per-node attributes & summaries, (6) bulk-persist. Retrieval is a separate subsystem (search/search.py) that runs edge/node/episode/community searches in parallel, each with pluggable search methods and a reranker, assembled from "recipes" in search_config_recipes.py. DB access is abstracted behind a GraphDriver with an optional `graph_operations_interface`/`search_interface` IoC layer (Zep cloud override hook) — almost every method has a `try interface; except NotImplementedError: fall back to Cypher` pattern.
data_model: EntityEdge (graphiti_core/edges.py:263-285) is the memory unit. Bi-temporal model uses FOUR timestamps, all persisted via edge_db_queries.py:91-99: `created_at` (system/transaction time the edge row was written), `expired_at` (system time the edge was marked no-longer-current), `valid_at` (event/valid time the fact became true), `invalid_at` (event time the fact stopped being true). A fifth field `reference_time` (edges.py:280-282) stores the source episode's valid_at. So created_at/expired_at = transaction-time axis; valid_at/invalid_at = valid-time axis — a genuine bitemporal design. Edge also carries `name` (SCREAMING_SNAKE relation), `fact` (NL sentence, the embedded text), `fact_embedding`, `episodes` (provenance UUID list), `attributes` (free dict, schema-driven). NOTABLE: EntityNode (nodes.py:499-504) has NO temporal fields at all — only name, name_embedding, summary, attributes, created_at (inherited). All temporality lives on edges; nodes are mutated in place (summary/attributes overwritten), so node history is NOT versioned. EpisodicNode (nodes.py:318-332) stores raw content + valid_at and is immutable. Storage: relationship properties in Neo4j/FalkorDB/Neptune; on Kuzu edges are reified into a `RelatesToNode_` node because Kuzu can't put rich props/vectors on rels (edges.py:318-322,388-391) and attributes are JSON-stringified.

key_files.role: Top-level orchestrator; add_episode (980-1228) and add_episode_bulk (1230-1487) define the write pipeline; search/search_ (1526-1629) the read entry
Edge extraction (extract_edges 117-322), dedup+invalidation (resolve_extracted_edge 623-847), temporal contradiction logic (resolve_edge_contradictions 538-573)
Entity extraction (extract_nodes 70-149), three-stage dedup (resolve_extracted_nodes 627-708), attribute+summary hydration (extract_attributes_from_nodes 726-780)
Deterministic dedup: exact-normalize, Shannon-entropy gate, MinHash+LSH+Jaccard fuzzy matching (whole file)
Parallel edge/node/episode/community search + reranker dispatch (rrf/mmr/cross_encoder/node_distance/episode_mentions)
Concrete BM25/cosine/BFS Cypher queries + rrf (1780), node_distance_reranker (1798), maximal_marginal_relevance (1901)
Edge extraction + standalone timestamp extraction + attribute extraction prompts
resolve_edge prompt: returns duplicate_facts + contradicted_facts index lists
Batch node dedup prompt (nodes 117-179) returning duplicate_candidate_id per entity
Cypher SET clauses that persist the 4 timestamps + reference_time
LLM-as-reranker via boolean True/False classification + logprobs
Opt-in temporal/date filters (valid_at/invalid_at/expired_at) — NOT applied by default

write_path: add_episode (graphiti.py:1067-1179) per single episode. Step 0: retrieve previous_episodes for context — last RELEVANT_SCHEMA_LIMIT=10 episodes (graphiti.py:1087-1096). Step 1 EXTRACT NODES: extract_nodes (node_operations.py:70-149) -> one LLM call, prompt chosen by episode source type message/text/json (_call_extraction_llm 255-280). Returns ExtractedEntity list with entity_type_id + episode_indices. Then `_collapse_exact_duplicate_extracted_nodes` (336-384) merges same-normalized-name dupes in-batch (keeps more specific label). Step 2 RESOLVE NODES: resolve_extracted_nodes (627-708). Per extracted node it runs a direct cosine `node_similarity_search` (NODE_DEDUP_CANDIDATE_LIMIT=15, NODE_DEDUP_COSINE_MIN_SCORE=0.6, node_operations.py:64-65,418-450) — NOT reranked. Then a deterministic pass `_resolve_with_similarity` (dedup_helpers.py:220-279): exact normalized-name hit -> auto-merge; ambiguous (>1) -> escalate; else entropy gate (_has_high_entropy, threshold 1.5, min len 6) then MinHash(32 perms, band size 4)/LSH/Jaccard with `_FUZZY_JACCARD_THRESHOLD=0.9` -> auto-merge. Only nodes still unresolved go to ONE batched LLM call `_resolve_with_llm` (467-624) using dedupe_nodes.nodes prompt. Step 3 EXTRACT EDGES: extract_edges (edge_operations.py:117-322) -> one LLM call (max_tokens 16384) producing triples constrained to extracted entity NAMES; invalid names dropped (218-230), self-edges dropped (232-240). valid_at/invalid_at can come straight from this prompt. Step 4 RESOLVE EDGES: resolve_extracted_edges (325-535): in-batch exact dedup on (src,tgt,normalized_fact) (345-358); embed all facts; for each edge fetch get_between_nodes + run TWO hybrid searches (EDGE_HYBRID_SEARCH_RRF) — one scoped to same-endpoint edges (duplicate candidates), one global (invalidation candidates) (392-418); then per-edge resolve_extracted_edge (623-847): fast verbatim-fact-match short circuit (684-695), else ONE LLM call dedupe_edges.resolve_edge returning duplicate_facts + contradicted_facts indices. If no timestamps yet, a SEPARATE small-model LLM call `_extract_edge_timestamps` (576-621). If a custom edge type matches, ANOTHER small-model LLM attribute call (788-794). Step 5 HYDRATE NODES: extract_attributes_from_nodes (726-780) — per-node parallel attribute LLM calls + batched summary LLM calls (MAX_NODES=30 per flight, 893-910). Step 6 PERSIST: add_nodes_and_edges_bulk. LLM CALL COUNT per episode is therefore variable and high: ~1 (extract nodes) + (0-1 batched node dedup) + 1 (extract edges) + N_edges*(1 resolve + up to 1 timestamp + up to 1 attribute) + N_nodes*(1 attribute) + ceil(N_summarize/30) summary calls — i.e. dozens of serial-ish LLM calls per episode for a busy episode.
read_path: search() (search.py:98-250) embeds the query once (only if any cosine/MMR method is configured, else zero-vector, 145-152) then runs edge_search/node_search/episode_search/community_search concurrently via semaphore_gather. Each scope: run the configured search methods, each returning 2*limit candidates (e.g. edge_search.py:284-311). Edge methods: bm25 (edge_fulltext_search, lucene-sanitized fulltext, search_utils.py:185), cosine (edge_similarity_search:300, min_score DEFAULT 0.6), bfs (edge_bfs_search:448, graph expansion from origin nodes up to bfs_max_depth, MAX_SEARCH_DEPTH=3). Candidates from all methods are unioned into a uuid->obj map, then a reranker fuses: RRF (search_utils.py:1780-1795, score = sum 1/(rank+rank_const), rank_const=1); MMR (1901-1939, builds full NxN cosine sim matrix in numpy, mmr = lambda*sim(q,d) + (lambda-1)*max_sim_to_selected, DEFAULT_MMR_LAMBDA=0.5, loads embeddings via get_embeddings_for_edges); cross_encoder (only top `limit` candidates by map order are reranked, 396-410); node_distance (seeds with RRF then re-sorts by shortest-hop distance to center_node_uuid via a Cypher 1-hop match, 1798-1857 — note it only counts DIRECT neighbors, non-neighbors get inf); episode_mentions (sorts by count of MENTIONS edges / len(edge.episodes)). Final slice reranked[:limit]. The convenience `graphiti.search()` returns only edges, using EDGE_HYBRID_SEARCH_RRF (or NODE_DISTANCE if center node given). `search_()` default recipe is COMBINED_HYBRID_SEARCH_CROSS_ENCODER. Token budget: there is NO token-budget/`max_tokens` packing of results — retrieval returns objects, caller is responsible for formatting/truncation. No reranking score normalization across rerankers.
conflict_resolution_impl: Two-layer, both on edges only. LAYER 1 (LLM semantic) — resolve_extracted_edge (edge_operations.py:623-847): the dedupe_edges.resolve_edge prompt (prompts/dedupe_edges.py:43-100) is given the new fact, a list of same-endpoint EXISTING FACTS (duplicate candidates) and a list of global INVALIDATION CANDIDATES, with CONTINUOUS indices across both lists, and returns `duplicate_facts` (must be from existing-facts range only) and `contradicted_facts` (from either list). Duplicates: resolved_edge becomes the existing edge, new episode UUID appended (744-752). Contradictions: matched existing edges become invalidation_candidates (770-776). LAYER 2 (deterministic temporal) — two pieces: (a) lines 826-839, if invalidation candidate has a later valid_at than the new edge, the NEW edge is itself expired (invalid_at=candidate.valid_at, expired_at=now) — i.e. incoming older info doesn't override newer; (b) resolve_edge_contradictions (538-573): for each contradicted existing edge, if existing.valid_at < new.valid_at, set existing.invalid_at = new.valid_at and existing.expired_at = now. There are interval-overlap guards (553-562) to skip edges whose validity windows don't actually conflict. CRITICAL DETAIL: invalidation is a soft, append-only "expire" — the contradicted edge is NOT deleted; it gets expired_at/invalid_at set and is re-saved (entity_edges = resolved + invalidated, graphiti.py:1156). So the graph keeps a full audit trail of superseded facts. The standalone add_triplet path reuses the same resolve_extracted_edge (graphiti.py:1740-1757).
forgetting_impl: No decay, no TTL, no consolidation/forgetting of facts. Invalidation is the only mechanism and it is non-destructive: contradicted edges are stamped expired_at/invalid_at and retained (edge_operations.py:564-571, 822-839). IMPORTANT and easily-missed: invalidated/expired edges are NOT filtered out at read time by default — search_filters.py shows valid_at/invalid_at/expired_at are all OPTIONAL filters (search_filters.py:62-65, applied only if the caller populates them, 149-271). So a default `graphiti.search()` returns stale/contradicted facts alongside current ones unless the caller explicitly passes a SearchFilters with an expired_at/invalid_at constraint. The only true deletions are explicit: remove_episode (graphiti.py:1765-1793) deletes edges whose first-provenance episode is the removed one and entities mentioned ONLY by that episode; build_communities/remove_communities rebuild community layer. Node summaries/attributes are overwritten (lossy) on each episode that touches them, but that's mutation not forgetting. Saga summaries are incremental with watermarks (graphiti.py:438-568) — a consolidation mechanism but for the Saga layer, not entity forgetting.

borrowable.pattern: Bitemporal edge with 4 timestamps + reference_time, invalidation as non-destructive expire
borrowable.why_good: Clean separation of transaction-time (created_at/expired_at) and valid-time (valid_at/invalid_at) lets you answer both 'what did we believe then' and 'what was true then'. Keeping superseded facts gives a free audit trail.

borrowable.pattern: Tiered dedup: cheap deterministic before expensive LLM
borrowable.why_good: Exact-normalized-name + entropy-gated MinHash/LSH/Jaccard (threshold 0.9) resolves the easy majority for free; only genuinely ambiguous entities hit a single BATCHED LLM call. Big cost/latency saver and deterministic.

borrowable.pattern: Entropy gate before fuzzy matching
borrowable.why_good: Shannon-entropy + length/token thresholds prevent unreliable fuzzy merges on short/low-information names (e.g. 'it', 'the app'), routing them to the LLM instead — a simple guard against false-positive merges.

borrowable.pattern: Pluggable hybrid search assembled from named recipes + RRF fusion
borrowable.why_good: Per-scope (edge/node/episode/community) independent search-method sets and rerankers, fused by parameter-free RRF, make it trivial to tune retrieval without touching query code. RRF needs no score calibration across heterogeneous signals.

borrowable.pattern: LLM-as-cross-encoder via boolean classification + logprobs
borrowable.why_good: Reranks without a dedicated cross-encoder model: asks a cheap model True/False for relevance, uses exp(logprob) as score, with logit_bias pinning the two tokens. Pragmatic when you only have an LLM endpoint.

borrowable.pattern: Dual-candidate-set conflict prompt with continuous indices
borrowable.why_good: Sending duplicate candidates and invalidation candidates in one prompt with a shared index space lets one LLM call decide both 'is this the same fact' and 'does this contradict' — and the few-shot teaches the subtle 'same relationship, updated value = contradiction not duplicate' distinction.

borrowable.pattern: Provenance via episodes[] on edges + MENTIONS edges
borrowable.why_good: Every fact links back to the episode(s) that asserted it, enabling source attribution and safe deletion (remove_episode only deletes facts/entities uniquely owned by an episode).

landmines.issue: Expired/invalidated edges are returned by default search. invalidation only sets expired_at/invalid_at; search_filters apply temporal constraints ONLY if the caller explicitly passes them, so a naive `graphiti.search(query)` surfaces stale and contradicted facts mixed with current ones.

High and unbounded LLM-call count per episode (extract nodes + batched node dedup + extract edges + per-edge resolve/timestamp/attribute + per-node attribute + summary flights). A content-rich episode can trigger dozens of serial LLM calls -> latency and cost scale with extracted entity/edge count.
Entity nodes are mutated in place — summary and attributes are overwritten each episode (lossy). No versioning of node state; only edges are temporal. You cannot reconstruct a past entity summary.

node_distance_reranker only matches DIRECT (1-hop) neighbors of the center node; everything else gets score inf and is ordered arbitrarily after neighbors. It is not a real shortest-path/multi-hop distance despite the name/docstring.

MMR builds a full O(n^2) dense similarity matrix in Python/numpy over all candidates and loads every candidate embedding from the DB first; fine for ~20 candidates, will not scale to large candidate pools.

Cross-encoder reranker fires one LLM request PER passage concurrently (semaphore_gather over all passages) and the OpenAI variant hardcodes token ids in logit_bias {'6432','7983'} that are model/tokenizer-specific — brittle across models.

Edge extraction rejects any triple whose source/target name isn't an exact string match to an extracted entity name (no fuzzy match), silently dropping facts when the edge LLM paraphrases an entity name.

group_id is overloaded as the physical database name: passing a group_id clones the driver to a DB of that name (self.driver.clone(database=group_id)). Multi-tenant partitioning is conflated with DB selection and mutates shared self.driver state mid-call.

Dedup candidate retrieval for nodes uses raw cosine top-15 at min_score 0.6 with NO reranking; recall of the dedup candidate set is entirely dependent on name-embedding quality, and a missed candidate becomes a duplicate entity in the graph.

production_readiness: Mid-to-high maturity for a research-derived OSS project, but not turnkey-prod for a memory product. POSITIVES: it is the production core of a commercial product (Zep), multi-backend (Neo4j/FalkorDB/Kuzu/Neptune) behind a clean GraphDriver + IoC interface; OpenTelemetry tracing is woven through ingestion and search (graphiti.py:1084, search.py:_trace_phase); telemetry, token tracking, rate-limit/retry error types, and per-provider LLM/embedder/reranker clients exist; defensive guardrails around LLM output are pervasive (index-range validation, missing/extra-id handling, malformed-response tolerance in _resolve_with_llm node_operations.py:560-624 and resolve_extracted_edge 736-767). Code quality is good: typed, documented, Pydantic models throughout. TEST COVERAGE: ~40 test files but the heavy ones (test_graphiti_int.py, test_edge_int.py, eval_e2e_graph_building.py) are integration/eval tests requiring a live DB + real LLM keys; unit coverage of the genuinely tricky logic (resolve_edge_contradictions interval math, MinHash dedup, rrf/mmr) is thin relative to its importance — much correctness rides on LLM behavior and is validated by evals, not deterministic unit tests. SCALABILITY CONCERNS for a real product: (1) write amplification — dozens of LLM calls per episode make high-throughput ingestion expensive/slow and put correctness at the mercy of model quality; (2) default retrieval returns invalidated facts, so you MUST build a temporal filter layer yourself; (3) MMR/cross-encoder rerankers are O(n^2)/O(n) LLM-calls and only viable at small candidate counts; (4) node state is non-versioned/lossy. RESEARCH-vs-PROD verdict: the bitemporal edge model, tiered dedup, and hybrid-search-with-RRF are prod-grade patterns worth borrowing wholesale. The forgetting/staleness story, the per-episode LLM cost, and the in-place node mutation are the areas you would have to redesign for a real personal-memory product. Treat the algorithms as a strong reference architecture, not a drop-in runtime.
notable_prompts: graphiti_core/prompts/extract_edges.py:94-176 `edge` — extracts fact triples constrained to a provided ENTITIES name list; the bulk of the prompt is anti-generalization rules (preserve brand names, counts, colors; 'Gamecube' must not become 'gaming console') and DATETIME rules for setting valid_at/invalid_at from REFERENCE_TIME. Drives extraction quality + first-pass temporal stamping.

graphiti_core/prompts/dedupe_edges.py:43-100 `resolve_edge` — the conflict-resolution brain: returns duplicate_facts (existing-range only) and contradicted_facts (either list) over a continuous index space; few-shot explicitly teaches 'same relationship but updated title = contradiction, NOT duplicate' (lines 90-92). This single prompt is what powers fact invalidation.

graphiti_core/prompts/dedupe_nodes.py:117-179 `nodes` — batched entity resolution; forces EXACTLY N resolutions for N inputs (lines 152-153) and uses semantic-equivalence few-shots (NYC=New York City; Java-lang != Java-island; Marco's car=Marco's vehicle). Only invoked for entities the deterministic pass couldn't resolve.

graphiti_core/prompts/extract_edges.py:181-239 `extract_attributes` — heavy 'HARD RULES' prompt to stop the model writing reasoning/justification/null-descriptions into attribute fields; reflects real-world LLM failure modes when filling typed schemas.

graphiti_core/prompts/extract_edges.py:242-271 `extract_timestamps` — small-model standalone valid_at/invalid_at extractor used when the main edge prompt didn't resolve dates; 'NEVER hallucinate dates' + relative-expression resolution from REFERENCE TIME.

graphiti_core/cross_encoder/openai_reranker_client.py:61-80 reranker prompt — minimal 'Respond True if PASSAGE relevant to QUERY else False', scored by token logprobs; turns a generic LLM into a relevance reranker.
storage_impl: Property graph. Default Neo4jDriver (graphiti.py:212); also FalkorDB, Kuzu, Neptune behind GraphDriver. Entities are :Entity nodes, episodes :Episodic, communities :Community, sagas :Saga. Facts are :RELATES_TO relationships carrying name/fact/fact_embedding/episodes/created_at/expired_at/valid_at/invalid_at/reference_time/attributes (edge_db_queries.py:91-99). Provenance via (:Episodic)-[:MENTIONS]->(:Entity). Embeddings stored inline on nodes (name_embedding) and edges (fact_embedding); vector + fulltext indexes built via build_indices_and_constraints (graphiti.py:570-602). Kuzu is special-cased: edges are reified to :RelatesToNode_ nodes (n)-[:RELATES_TO]->(RelatesToNode_)-[:RELATES_TO]->(m) and attributes JSON-stringified, because Kuzu lacks rich relationship properties (edges.py:318-322,358-363). On non-Kuzu backends, free-form edge `attributes` are spread as top-level relationship properties (edges.py:365-371) and stripped back out on read (get_entity_edge_from_record edges.py:974-986). group_id doubles as the database name (driver.clone(database=group_id), graphiti.py:1079-1082).

architecture_overview: The memory core is split across pydantic schemas, ORM models, and "manager" services. A `Memory` object (letta/schemas/memory.py:68) holds a list of `Block` objects; `Memory.compile()` (memory.py:688) renders those blocks into an XML-ish `<memory_blocks>` string concatenated into the system prompt at request-build time. Blocks are persisted independently as DB rows (orm/block.py) and linked to agents via a `blocks_agents` pivot, so one block can be shared across many agents; editing a block triggers `_rebuild_system_prompts_for_connected_agents` (block_manager.py:61). Agent memory-edit tools are defined as plain Python function stubs in letta/functions/function_sets/base.py (most raise NotImplementedError — they are schema/docstring carriers), and the REAL behavior lives in letta/services/tool_executor/core_tool_executor.py, which mutates the in-memory `Memory` object then calls `update_memory_if_changed_async`. Archival memory is a separate subsystem: `Archive` rows own `Passage` rows (with embeddings), via ArchiveManager/PassageManager, optionally dual-written to Turbopuffer. Overflow handling (eviction/summarization) lives in letta/services/summarizer/ and is driven by the agent loop (letta/agents/letta_agent_v3.py) on a token threshold. A sleep-time agent (a second full LettaAgent) is invoked every N turns by letta/groups/sleeptime_multi_agent_v4.py to reorganize core blocks.
data_model:
CORE MEMORY (in-context): `Block` (letta/schemas/block.py:67, ORM orm/block.py) — id, label (str, e.g. "human"/"persona"; path-like "system/persona" for git agents), value (content str), limit (int char limit, default CORE_MEMORY_BLOCK_CHAR_LIMIT=100000), description (what the block is FOR), read_only (bool), metadata (dict), tags (List[str] via blocks_tags), hidden, plus template fields. Optimistic concurrency via current_history_entry_id pointer into a `BlockHistory` table (block_manager.py:842-911) giving per-block linear undo/redo (sequence_number). Blocks↔agents many-to-many via `blocks_agents`. FileBlock (block.py:107) is a Block subclass representing an open file in context.

ARCHIVAL MEMORY (out-of-context): `Archive` (one per agent today; code rejects >1, archive_manager.py:251) owns `ArchivalPassage` rows. Passage fields: id, archive_id, organization_id, text, embedding (pgvector or sqlite blob), embedding_config, tags (List[str]), metadata, created_at. `archives_agents` pivot links archives to agents (is_owner). Archive.vector_db_provider = NATIVE (pgvector/SQLite) or TPUF (Turbopuffer) + optional _vector_db_namespace.

RECALL MEMORY: just the full `Message` table for the agent, searched via message_manager (hybrid text+semantic). No separate structure.

key_files.role: Memory + Block container; compile() renders core memory into the system prompt (standard / line-numbered / git-tree variants); archival search result schemas
Block pydantic schema: value/limit/label/description/read_only/tags; no hard limit-enforcement validator

Tool DEFINITIONS (docstrings = LLM-facing prompts) for core_memory_append/replace, memory_replace/insert/rethink/apply_patch/finish_edits, archival_memory_insert/search, conversation_search. Most are NotImplementedError stubs
ACTUAL implementations of memory tools (278-700+): core_memory_*, memory_replace/insert/apply_patch, archival_memory_search/insert
Block CRUD, tag pivots, system-prompt rebuild on change, BlockHistory checkpoint/undo/redo
Archive lifecycle, passage create/delete, default-archive-per-agent, Turbopuffer dual-write
insert_passage(): embeds text and writes passage rows (no chunking — whole content = 1 passage)
search_agent_archival_memory_async (2534) + query_agent_passages_async (2416): archival read path / ranking
build_agent_passage_query: native pgvector cosine_distance ranking SQL (~988)
compact_messages(): LLM summarization of in-context messages, mode dispatch + fallbacks
legacy Summarizer: FIFO static-buffer + partial-evict; simple_summary() with 3-tier truncation fallback
get_compaction_trigger_threshold = context_window * 0.9
Main loop; triggers compaction when context_token_estimate > 0.9*context_window (1439-1474) and on ContextWindowExceededError (1218-1247)
Fires sleep-time agent every N turns (turns_counter % frequency); builds transcript + system-reminder prompt
Sleep-time agent system prompt (memory-manager persona)
ALL_PROMPT / SLIDING_PROMPT / SELF_* summarization prompts

write_path:
CORE MEMORY WRITE (synchronous, LLM-driven). LLM emits a tool call; core_tool_executor.py runs it against the live `Memory` object then persists. NO extraction/dedup pipeline — the LLM decides what/how to write.
- `core_memory_append` (core_tool_executor.py:319): read_only check, `new_value = current_value + "\n" + content`, update_block_value, then update_memory_if_changed_async. Pure string append.
- `core_memory_replace` (328): read_only check; raises if old_content not present; `value.replace(old,new)`.
- `memory_replace` (346): precise editor for line-numbered (Anthropic) agents. Rejects line-number prefixes + warning banner via regex (357-374), expandtabs(), requires old_string UNIQUE (count==0 -> error; count>1 -> error listing line numbers, 380-391). This uniqueness rule is the only conflict guard.
- `memory_insert` (683) inserts at a line index; `memory_apply_patch` applies a codex-style multi-block unified diff (create/update/delete/rename); `memory_rethink`/`rethink_memory` overwrite a whole block.
After mutation, update_memory_if_changed_async (agent_manager.py:1747) diffs+persists changed blocks and rebuilds the system prompt. Edits also create BlockHistory checkpoints (block_manager.py:842).

ARCHIVAL WRITE (core_tool_executor.archival_memory_insert:307 -> passage_manager.insert_passage:543): get-or-create the agent's single default archive; `text_chunks = [text]` (NO chunking — whole content = one passage, passage_manager.py:567); embed; write one passage row to SQL; if TPUF, dual-write reusing same id+embedding (606-628, failures swallowed unless strict_mode). Then rebuild_system_prompt_async(force=True) to update the archival-size counter. No dedup, no cross-passage conflict detection — archival is append-only.

SLEEP-TIME (async rewrite): sleeptime_multi_agent_v4.run_sleeptime_agents (132) bumps a turns_counter; when turns_counter % sleeptime_agent_frequency == 0 it spawns _participant_agent_step (202) as a safe_create_task. That builds a transcript of messages since last_processed_message_id, wraps it in a <system-reminder> framing the agent as a background memory manager (233-243), and runs a full LettaAgentV3 step whose tools are the precise memory editors. The rewrite is itself just another LLM calling core_memory_replace/memory_rethink.
read_path:
CORE MEMORY READ: zero-cost — blocks are always in the system prompt. Memory.compile() (memory.py:688) picks a renderer by agent type + provider: `_render_memory_blocks_standard` (143) emits per block `<label><description>..</description><metadata>read_only / chars_current / chars_limit</metadata><value>..</value></label>`; for Anthropic + memgpt_v2/sleeptime/letta_v1 it uses `_render_memory_blocks_line_numbered` (175) prefixing each line with `N→ ` plus CORE_MEMORY_LINE_NUMBER_WARNING (view-only; edit tools reject them); git agents use `_render_memory_blocks_git` (205) rendering a nested file-tree. compile_async offloads to a thread (735).

ARCHIVAL READ (semantic search). archival_memory_search -> agent_manager.search_agent_archival_memory_async (2534): parses ISO start/end datetimes (date-only -> start/end-of-day + agent timezone, 2574-2610), maps tag_match_mode, limit = top_k or RETRIEVAL_QUERY_DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE (=5, constants.py:458), then query_agent_passages_async (2416). Ranking:
- TPUF archive: tpuf_client.query_passages(search_mode='hybrid', top_k, tags, date filters) — hybrid vector+full-text, returns (passage, score, metadata) (2457-2476).
- NATIVE archive: build_agent_passage_query (agent_manager_helper.py) embeds the query and orders by pgvector embedding.cosine_distance(embedded_text).asc() on Postgres (992) or a custom cosine_distance func on SQLite (998). Tags are POST-filtered in Python AFTER the SQL fetch (agent_manager.py:2510-2527, flagged TODO), and SQL-path scores are returned as 0.0.
NO reranking, NO token-budget packing of results, NO RRF/score fusion in the native path (hybrid fusion exists only inside the Turbopuffer client). Results formatted with agent-timezone timestamps + tags into ArchivalMemorySearchResult.

RECALL READ: conversation_search (base.py:87) / list_messages_for_agent — hybrid text+semantic over the Message table, default limit 5.

CONTEXT BUDGET: agent tracks context_token_estimate from last LLM usage; when > 0.9*context_window it compacts (see forgetting_impl).
conflict_resolution_impl:
No semantic conflict detection. Resolution is delegated to the LLM via prompt instructions plus a few mechanical guards:
1. core_memory_replace / memory_replace require an exact substring match of old_content/old_string; memory_replace additionally requires the match be UNIQUE, erroring with offending line numbers if multiple (core_tool_executor.py:380-391). The only ambiguity rejection.
2. memory_rethink / rethink_memory (base.py:283,488) instruct the model to produce a block integrating new info and dropping 'outdated or inconsistent' content — resolution is the model's job, prompted by sleeptime_v2.py ('not redundant and outdate information', absolute dates not 'today/recently').
3. The sleep-time agent is the dedicated reconciler: re-reads the conversation and rewrites blocks (sleeptime_multi_agent_v4.py:233-243).
4. DB-level: optimistic locking (version_id_col) + BlockHistory checkpoints (block_manager.py:842) prevent silent clobber, but this is row-versioning, not content reconciliation.
5. Archival has NO conflict handling — append-only; contradictory passages can coexist and both be returned by search.
forgetting_impl:
Two mechanisms, both for the CONVERSATION buffer (not the memory stores):

1. LLM SUMMARIZATION / COMPACTION (primary, current). letta_agent_v3.py post-step check (1439): if context_token_estimate > get_compaction_trigger_threshold = context_window * SUMMARIZATION_TRIGGER_MULTIPLIER (=0.9, thresholds.py:41 / constants.py:83), or on ContextWindowExceededError mid-request (1218-1247), call compact() -> compact_messages (compact.py:134). Modes: 'all', 'sliding_window', 'self_compact_all', 'self_compact_sliding_window' — each summarizes evicted messages via an LLM (summarizer model often routed to a cheaper model e.g. Haiku 4.5 for auto agents, compact.py:67) and rebuilds context as [system] + [summary role=summary] + remaining (462-465). compact_messages verifies post-compaction tokens < threshold and cascades fallbacks (sliding_window -> all; raises SystemPromptTokenExceededError if even system prompt overflows, 398-407). simple_summary (summarizer.py:488) has a 3-tier ContextWindowExceeded fallback: full transcript -> clamp tool returns -> middle-truncate to a byte budget (~0.6*context_window*4).

2. FIFO / PARTIAL-EVICT BUFFER (legacy Summarizer). STATIC_MESSAGE_BUFFER: keep message_buffer_min recent, evict the rest, fire-and-forget a background summary (summarizer.py:244-343). PARTIAL_EVICT: evict ~30% (partial_evict_summarizer_percentage) from index 1 to first assistant message, replace with a recursive summary at index 1 (136-242).

Neither core blocks NOR archival passages have decay/TTL/consolidation. Core blocks shrink only if the LLM/sleep-time agent deletes/replaces; archival is permanent/append-only (docstring: 'persists indefinitely', base.py:176). The `limit` field is advisory only.
storage_impl: Polyglot persistence behind async SQLAlchemy (Postgres prod, SQLite local). Core memory = `block` table (+ `blocks_agents`, `blocks_tags`, `block_history` for versioning). Archival = `archives` + `archival_passages` (+ `archives_agents`); passage embeddings in pgvector columns on Postgres or a custom blob/cosine_distance UDF on SQLite (agent_manager_helper.py:990-1002). Optional Turbopuffer external ANN index, dual-written with matching ids (passage_manager.py:606). Recall = the `messages` table. Managers use db_registry.async_session() context managers owning commits; access org-scoped via apply_access_predicate. Cursor-based pagination (created_at,id) throughout, with SQLite timestamp-rounding workarounds (block_manager.py:35-50,426-446).
notable_prompts: letta/prompts/system_prompts/sleeptime_v2.py — sleep-time/memory-manager system prompt: read-only vs read-write blocks, precise vs broad (rethink) edits, demands absolute dates not 'today/recently', rule that line numbers are view-only and must never appear in edit tool args

letta/groups/sleeptime_multi_agent_v4.py:233-243 — per-invocation <system-reminder> framing the sleep-time agent as a background reviewer ('You are NOT the primary agent'; assistant msgs are the primary agent, user msgs are its user) and telling it to update memory blocks with info worth preserving

letta/prompts/summarizer_prompt.py — ALL_PROMPT/SLIDING_PROMPT/SELF_* : structured 6-7 section summary (High level goals / What happened / Important details, preserve identifiers verbatim / Errors and fixes / Current state / Next step / Lookup hints), word-limited (500/300); SELF_* variants forbid tool use and conversation continuation

letta/functions/function_sets/base.py:164-243 — archival_memory_insert/search docstrings ARE the model-facing instructions: 'store self-contained facts not conversational fragments', 'add descriptive tags', 'query by concept/meaning not exact phrases', tag_match_mode any/all
letta/prompts/system_prompts/memgpt_v2_chat.py — core MemGPT persona/control-flow prompt explaining core vs recall vs archival memory and 'check context before searching'
letta/functions/function_sets/base.py:311-338 — memory_replace docstring with good/bad examples teaching the model NOT to include '1→' line prefixes or the warning banner in edit args

borrowable.pattern: Memory-as-prompt-fragment with self-describing blocks: each block carries a `description` ('what this block is for') and live chars_current/chars_limit metadata rendered inline so the model self-governs size
borrowable.why_good: Decouples storage from rendering; the description doubles as an instruction about when/how to write, and the live char counts nudge the model to compact before hitting limits — no separate enforcement code needed

borrowable.pattern: Line-numbered view + strict unique-substring replace editor that rejects line-number prefixes in tool args
borrowable.why_good: Gives the model precise, low-error edits (like a code editor's str_replace) while preventing the classic failure where it pastes back displayed line numbers; uniqueness turns ambiguous edits into recoverable errors

borrowable.pattern: Cascading summarization with explicit token-budget fallbacks (clamp tool returns -> middle-truncate to byte budget) and provider fallback (Anthropic->Bedrock)
borrowable.why_good: Production-grade robustness: never bricks the agent on ContextWindowExceeded; degrades gracefully and verifies the result is actually under threshold before returning

borrowable.pattern: Per-block version history with linear undo/redo via a BlockHistory table + current_history_entry_id pointer
borrowable.why_good: Cheap audit trail and rollback for agent-written memory; truncates future checkpoints on new write to keep a clean undo stack — reusable for 'who/what changed this memory' and recovery

borrowable.pattern: Shared blocks across agents with automatic dependent system-prompt rebuild on block change (only when prompt-visible fields change)
borrowable.why_good: Lets multiple agents (foreground + sleep-time) share a single source-of-truth block; PROMPT_AFFECTING_BLOCK_FIELDS diffing avoids needless prompt rebuilds

borrowable.pattern: Asynchronous 'sleep-time' reconciler agent that runs every N turns on the conversation delta, told via system-reminder it is NOT the primary agent
borrowable.why_good: Separates fast user turns from slower memory consolidation; reuses the same agent/tool machinery rather than a bespoke pipeline, and the framing stops the reconciler from replying to the user

borrowable.pattern: Dual-write to SQL + vector DB with the same primary key, SQL as source of truth and vector errors swallowed
borrowable.why_good: Durable truth in Postgres plus fast ANN from Turbopuffer; shared id makes reconciliation straightforward and a vector outage doesn't lose data

landmines.issue: Block `limit` (default 100000 chars) is rendered as chars_limit metadata but NOT enforced server-side on write. core_memory_append/replace and memory_replace just assign the new value; bulk_update_block_values_async docstring claims it raises if value exceeds limit but the implementation only does block.value = new_val with no check. A misbehaving model can blow up the context window.

archival_memory_insert stores the entire content as a SINGLE passage with no chunking (text_chunks=[text]). Large inserts produce one giant passage that embeds poorly and can exceed the embedding model's token limit (there is even a 'TODO: check token count' comment).

Native (non-TPUF) archival search returns relevance score 0.0 for every result and post-filters tags in Python AFTER applying the LIMIT — so tag-filtered native searches can silently return fewer than top_k (or zero) results even when matching passages exist beyond the limit window.
Default RETRIEVAL_QUERY_DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE is only 5 — archival/recall searches return very few results by default, which can starve recall on large stores unless the model passes an explicit top_k.
Hard one-archive-per-agent assumption: several paths raise ValueError('multiple archives, not yet supported'). Multi-archive needs rework.

Sleep-time consolidation runs fire-and-forget via safe_create_task with no durable retry/queue; if the process dies the memory update for that turn is lost (only last_processed_message_id advances on success).
Turbopuffer dual-write failures are swallowed (logged, not raised) unless strict_mode=True, so SQL and the vector index can silently diverge.

Agent-facing tool definitions in function_sets/base.py are decoys (NotImplementedError); real logic is in core_tool_executor.py — easy to read the wrong file. Two near-identical copies of _move_block_to_sequence also exist in block_manager.py (760 and 914).

production_readiness: More production-grade than typical research memory repos, but with real gaps. Strengths: async SQLAlchemy with org-scoped access control, optimistic locking + block version history, cursor pagination, Postgres+SQLite dialect handling, pgvector/Turbopuffer abstraction, extensive multi-tier summarization fallbacks with provider failover, OTEL tracing (@trace_method) + LLM-call telemetry, and large dedicated tests (test_block_manager.py ~1516 LOC, test_archive_manager.py ~1221 LOC, integration_test_summarizer.py ~2290 LOC). Weaknesses for a real product: (1) memory correctness is almost entirely delegated to the LLM — no dedup, no semantic conflict detection, append-only archival, advisory-only char limits not enforced on write; (2) native (non-Turbopuffer) archival search is weak — score 0.0, tag post-filtering after LIMIT, default top_k=5, no reranking/fusion; (3) one-archive-per-agent hard constraint; (4) sleep-time consolidation is fire-and-forget with no durable job queue/retry; (5) heavy iteration debt — letta_agent v1/v2/v3, summarizer legacy + compact + sliding_window + self_*, sleeptime_multi_agent v2/v3/v4, duplicated methods, and large commented-out blocks in the hot path (letta_agent_v3.py:360-420), plus decoy tool stubs. Net: borrow the architecture and the editing/summarization patterns, but build your own retrieval quality, conflict resolution, limit enforcement, and consolidation durability.

system: cognee — open-source "memory layer for AI agents" that turns raw data into a knowledge graph + vector index via an ECL (Extract-Cognify-Load) pipeline. Repo: /Users/coby/chanyoung/memsys-refsrc/cognee. The "memory core" is the cognify pipeline (chunk -> LLM graph extraction -> ontology validation -> node/edge dedup -> graph+vector persistence) plus the graph_completion retriever (vector-seeded brute-force triplet ranking).
architecture_overview:
cognee is organized as a task-DAG pipeline framework, not a bespoke memory service. The public API (cognee/api/v1/cognify/cognify.py) builds a list of Task objects and hands them to run_pipeline. Three storage backends are abstracted behind a "unified engine" (cognee/infrastructure/databases/unified): a graph DB (Neo4j/Kuzu/NetworkX), a vector DB (LanceDB/pgvector/Chroma/etc.), and a relational DB (SQLite/Postgres) for ownership/permissions/pipeline-status bookkeeping. Some backends (NeptuneAnalytics, Postgres-hybrid) implement a HYBRID_WRITE capability that writes nodes+vectors atomically.

The core abstraction is the DataPoint (cognee/infrastructure/engine/models/DataPoint.py): a Pydantic BaseModel that every domain object (DocumentChunk, Entity, EntityType, Event, Triplet) subclasses. A DataPoint instance IS simultaneously the relational record, the graph node, AND the vector-index source — the framework walks the Pydantic field graph to derive nodes/edges (get_graph_from_model.py) and uses metadata["index_fields"] to decide which fields get embedded (index_data_points.py).

The default cognify DAG (cognify.py get_default_tasks, lines 316-342): [classify_documents] -> [extract_chunks_from_documents] -> [extract_graph_and_summarize (LLM entity/relationship extraction + chunk summarization)] -> [add_data_points (persist nodes/edges/embeddings)] -> [extract_dlt_fk_edges]. A separate temporal DAG (get_temporal_tasks, lines 347-392) swaps the extraction step for event/timestamp extraction. memify is a SECOND pipeline that operates on the ALREADY-BUILT graph (or a subgraph "memory fragment") to add derived structure: triplet embeddings, session/feedback enrichment, and a "global context index" of bucketed summaries.

Retrieval (modules/retrieval/graph_completion_retriever.py) is RAG over the graph: embed the query, vector-search seed nodes across per-type collections, project a graph (or k-hop neighborhood), rank triplets by a blended distance/importance/feedback score, resolve top-k triplets to text, and feed to an LLM completion prompt.
data_model:
Core model: DataPoint (infrastructure/engine/models/DataPoint.py:27-63). Fields: id:UUID (default uuid4, but see identity logic), created_at/updated_at (int epoch-ms), version:int=1, ontology_valid:bool, topological_rank, metadata:MetaData(TypedDict with index_fields, identity_fields), type:str (auto-set to class name in __init__ line 68), belongs_to_set (nodeset membership), source_pipeline/source_task/source_user/source_content_hash (provenance), feedback_weight:float=0.5, importance_weight:float=0.5.

ID strategy is the load-bearing dedup mechanism: by default id=uuid4, BUT if a subclass declares metadata.identity_fields, __init__ (lines 65-76) calls _generate_identity_id (lines 104-131) which builds a deterministic uuid5(NAMESPACE_OID, "ClassName:field1|field2") after lowercasing/normalizing string values. Separately, graph entity nodes get IDs via generate_node_id (infrastructure/engine/utils/generate_node_id.py): uuid5(NAMESPACE_OID, name.lower().replace(' ','_').replace(chr(39),'')). So two mentions of "Albert Einstein" anywhere collapse to the same node ID — this is how entity coreference/merge actually works (deterministic name-hash, NOT semantic).

Domain subclasses are tiny: Entity (modules/engine/models/Entity.py): name, is_a:Optional[EntityType], description, relations:List[tuple], metadata index_fields=["name"]. EntityType: name, description, relations, index_fields=["name"]. DocumentChunk (modules/chunking/models/DocumentChunk.py): text, chunk_size, chunk_index, cut_type, is_part_of:Document, contains:List[Entity|Event|tuple[Edge,Entity]], importance_weight, index_fields=["text"]. Event/Timestamp/Interval in tasks/temporal_graph/models.py. Triplet (modules/engine/models/Triplet.py): from_node_id, to_node_id, text.

The LLM-facing extraction schema is separate (shared/data_models.py:49-77): a lightweight KnowledgeGraph{nodes:[Node{id,name,type,description}], edges:[Edge{source_node_id,target_node_id,relationship_name,description}]} — this is what the LLM returns, then expand_with_nodes_and_edges converts it into Entity/EntityType DataPoints.

Edges are NOT objects in storage; they are 4-tuples (source_id, target_id, relationship_name, properties_dict) produced by get_graph_from_model and persisted via graph_engine.add_edges.

key_files.role: ECL pipeline orchestration; get_default_tasks (line 316) and get_temporal_tasks (line 347) define the actual task DAGs
Core unified model; deterministic identity-ID generation (uuid5) is the dedup/merge primitive
Default extraction: parallel LLM calls per chunk, edge-validity filtering, ontology integration, provenance stamping
Cascade extraction variant: n-round nodes->relnames->triplets multi-pass extraction
Converts LLM KnowledgeGraph into Entity/EntityType DataPoints, ontology validation, builds contains edges
Walks Pydantic field graph of a DataPoint into (nodes, edges); LRU-cached model copying
LOAD step: dedup, ensure edge props, write nodes+edges+vectors (hybrid or split), optional triplet embeddings
Embeds index_fields per DataPoint type into per-(type,field) vector collections
Default search: triplet retrieval -> resolve to text -> LLM completion
Vector-seeded graph projection + triplet ranking core
In-memory graph projection; _calculate_query_top_triplet_importances (line 469) is the ranking algorithm
Deletion: dataset/data-item/memory-only/everything; no decay/TTL
Second-pass enrichment pipeline over existing graph
Streaming feedback-weight update (EMA) on graph nodes/edges
The actual entity/relationship extraction system prompt

storage_impl: Three stores behind a unified engine (infrastructure/databases/unified). Graph DB: Neo4j/Kuzu/NetworkX/NeptuneAnalytics adapters; nodes added via graph_engine.add_nodes(nodes) (DataPoint instances serialized), edges via add_edges(list of (src,tgt,rel,props) tuples). Vector DB: LanceDB (default)/pgvector/Chroma/etc.; one collection per (DataPoint type, index_field) e.g. Entity_name, DocumentChunk_text, Triplet_text, created lazily via create_vector_index (index_data_points.py:47). Relational DB: SQLite/Postgres holds Data/Dataset/DatasetData ownership, per-data pipeline_status JSON, users/tenants/permissions, and session/feedback cache (Redis or FS). Some adapters expose EngineCapability.HYBRID_WRITE so add_nodes_with_vectors/add_edges_with_vectors write graph+vector atomically (add_data_points.py:85-119); otherwise nodes and embeddings are written concurrently but non-atomically via asyncio.gather. Embeddings are produced by an embedding_engine with a batch_size; index_data_points batches per collection under an asyncio.Semaphore(4).
write_path:
Entry: cognify() -> get_default_tasks (cognify.py:316). The DAG runs per batch via run_pipeline.

1) classify_documents + extract_chunks_from_documents: raw Data -> typed Document -> DocumentChunk DataPoints. Chunk size = chunk_size or get_max_chunk_tokens(); chunker default TextChunker (paragraph-based).

2) extract_graph_and_summarize -> extract_graph_from_data (tasks/graph/extract_graph_from_data.py:128). For non-DLT chunks it fires one LLM call per chunk IN PARALLEL: asyncio.gather(*[extract_content_graph(chunk.text, graph_model, custom_prompt) ...]) (lines 166-173). extract_content_graph (infrastructure/llm/extraction/knowledge_graph/extract_content_graph.py:13) renders generate_graph_prompt.txt as the system prompt and calls LLMGateway.acreate_structured_output(content, system_prompt, KnowledgeGraph) — i.e. structured-output/function-calling to coerce a Pydantic KnowledgeGraph. There is NO retrieval of prior memory before extraction — each chunk is extracted in isolation, so cross-chunk consolidation happens ONLY via deterministic ID collision later.

   Edge sanitization (lines 181-188): edges whose source/target node id is not in the chunk's own node set are dropped.

3) integrate_chunk_graphs -> expand_with_nodes_and_edges (expand_with_nodes_and_edges.py:340). For each LLM node it creates an EntityType node (generate_node_id(node.type)) and an Entity node (generate_node_id(node.id)), runs ontology_resolver.get_subgraph for optional canonicalization (if a closest ontology class/individual matches, the node is RE-KEYED to the ontology term's id — this is the only "semantic" normalization, and it is off by default since default resolver matches nothing). It appends a (Edge(relationship_type='contains', edge_text='Document chunk mentions X: <desc>'), entity_node) tuple to chunk.contains (lines 263-278), and converts LLM edges into relationship tuples with edge_text=description (_process_graph_edges lines 281-311). retrieve_existing_edges (retrieve_existing_edges.py) pre-queries the graph DB via graph_engine.has_edges to skip edges that already exist (cross-run dedup at edge level).

4) add_data_points (tasks/storage/add_data_points.py:30). get_graph_from_model walks every DataPoint's Pydantic fields (lines 230-248): a field whose value is a DataPoint / list[DataPoint] / (Edge,DataPoint) tuple becomes a relationship+recursion; everything else becomes a node property. Then deduplicate_nodes_and_edges (deduplicate_nodes_and_edges.py) — pure dict-keyed dedup: nodes by str(id), edges by str(src)+str(rel)+str(tgt). Persistence: if HYBRID_WRITE capability, graph_engine.add_nodes_with_vectors(nodes) then add_edges_with_vectors; else asyncio.gather(graph.add_nodes, index_data_points(...)) and separately edges. index_data_points (index_data_points.py:10) groups DataPoints by (type_name, field_name), creates a vector collection named f"{TypeName}_{field}" (e.g. Entity_name, DocumentChunk_text), and embeds. If embed_triplets, _create_triplets_from_graph (add_data_points.py:184) builds "src -> rel -> tgt" text Triplet DataPoints and indexes them.

Provenance: _stamp_provenance_deep (extract_graph_from_data.py:30) recursively sets source_pipeline/source_task on reachable DataPoints (only if currently None).
read_path:
Entry: GraphCompletionRetriever.get_completion (graph_completion_retriever.py:349) -> get_retrieved_objects -> get_triplets -> brute_force_triplet_search.

1) Collections: _get_vector_index_collections (graph_completion_retriever.py:87) enumerates all DataPoint subclasses and builds "{Class}_{index_field}" names; brute_force_triplet_search.py:281 defaults to ["Entity_name","TextSummary_text","EntityType_name","DocumentChunk_text"] and always appends "EdgeType_relationship_name".

2) NodeEdgeVectorSearch.embed_and_retrieve_distances (brute_force_triplet_search.py:301): embeds the query once and runs vector search against each collection with limit=wide_search_top_k (default 100). node_name filter is supported (exact-match prefilter). Results are ScoredResult with .id and .score (cosine distance).

3) get_memory_fragment (brute_force_triplet_search.py:49) projects a CogneeGraph from the graph DB. Default properties_to_project = ["id","description","name","type","text","importance_weight"]. THREE projection modes (CogneeGraph.project_graph_from_db:225): nodeset subgraph (if node_type+node_name), neighborhood (k-hop from seed ids if neighborhood_depth set), or _get_full_or_id_filtered_graph. With wide_search_top_k set, relevant_node_ids = vector_search.extract_relevant_node_ids() filters the projection to those nodes; otherwise (wide_search_limit None) it projects the FULL graph — a real scale landmine.

4) map_vector_distances_to_graph_nodes/edges (CogneeGraph.py:404,439): attaches per-query vector_distance lists onto projected nodes/edges by id match; unmatched elements keep triplet_distance_penalty (default 6.5).

5) Ranking — _calculate_query_top_triplet_importances (CogneeGraph.py:469-539): for each edge, score = sum over {node1, node2, edge} of effective_distance, where per-element distance = (2 - importance_weight) * vector_distance[query_index], then blended with feedback_weight: blended = (1-fi)*(d/2) + fi*(1-feedback_weight), *2 (only when distance is a real cosine value in [0,2]; penalties pass through). Returns heapq.nsmallest(k, edges, key=score) — i.e. lower combined distance wins. importance_weight>1 reduces distance (boosts), feedback_weight near 1 reduces distance.

6) resolve_edges_to_text (modules/graph/utils/resolve_edges_to_text.py:61): builds a "Nodes:\n... Connections:\n src --[rel]--> tgt (edge_text)" block. Node names are synthesized via _create_title_from_text (first 7 words + top-3 frequent words) when a text field exists.

7) Completion: graph_context_for_question.txt user prompt injects {{question}} and {{context}}; system prompt answer_simple_question.txt ("Answer the question using the provided context. Be as brief as possible."). Optional session cache (Redis/FS) records used_graph_element_ids for later feedback. No explicit token-budget truncation in the retriever itself beyond top_k and an optional max_context_chars passed to the session manager.
conflict_resolution_impl:
There is NO semantic conflict resolution, contradiction detection, or LLM-based merge anywhere in the write path. "Conflict resolution" is entirely deterministic ID-collision dedup:

1) Entity merge: generate_node_id (generate_node_id.py) = uuid5(NAMESPACE_OID, name.lower().replace(' ','_').replace("'","")). Any two entities with the same normalized name get the same UUID, so re-cognifying or a second chunk mentioning the same entity coincides on one node. No fuzzy matching — "Einstein" and "Albert Einstein" are DIFFERENT nodes.

2) DataPoint identity: _generate_identity_id (DataPoint.py:104) = uuid5 over declared identity_fields, same idea for typed records.

3) Within a batch: deduplicate_nodes_and_edges (deduplicate_nodes_and_edges.py) dedups nodes by str(id) and edges by src+rel+tgt string concat. Keeps FIRST occurrence for nodes.

4) Cross-run edge dedup: retrieve_existing_edges queries the graph DB (graph_engine.has_edges) before insert and skips already-existing edges.

5) Ontology canonicalization (expand_with_nodes_and_edges _create_type_node/_create_entity_node) can re-key a node to a matched ontology class/individual id — the closest thing to entity resolution, but it requires a configured RDF ontology + matching strategy (default resolver matches nothing, so it is inactive out of the box).

Updates: DataPoint.update_version (DataPoint.py:242) bumps version and updated_at, but nothing in the cognify path calls it — versioning is effectively dead code. There is no "the user moved cities, retract the old fact" logic; an old fact and a new contradicting fact both persist as separate edges unless they collide on (src,rel,tgt).
forgetting_impl:
No decay, no TTL, no consolidation, no automatic invalidation. "Forgetting" is explicit, user-driven deletion only.

forget() (api/v1/forget/forget.py:16) is a unified delete with modes: data_item, dataset, dataset_memory_only, data_item_memory_only, everything. memory_only variants (_forget_dataset_memory line 231, _forget_data_memory line 304) call delete_dataset_nodes_and_edges / delete_data_nodes_and_edges (modules/graph/methods/delete_dataset_nodes_and_edges.py) which compute affected nodes/edges (scoped by dataset ownership records in the relational DB) and call delete_from_graph_and_vector to remove from BOTH graph and vector stores, then reset pipeline_status JSON so the dataset can be re-cognified. Raw files are preserved in memory_only mode. prune (api/v1/prune/prune.py) nukes everything (prune_data + prune_system).

There ARE weight signals that could drive forgetting but DON'T: feedback_weight (apply_feedback_weights.py — streaming EMA: new = prev + alpha*(normalized_rating - prev), alpha=0.1, rating 1..5 mapped to [0,1]) and frequency_weight / importance_weight only re-RANK retrieval (they multiply vector_distance in _calculate_query_top_triplet_importances). A 0-weight node is down-ranked, never deleted. So there is soft down-weighting but no true forgetting/garbage-collection.

borrowable.pattern: Single unified model (DataPoint) that simultaneously serves as relational record, graph node, and vector-embedding source via metadata.index_fields, with the framework deriving the graph by walking Pydantic fields.
borrowable.why_good: Eliminates the usual triple-write boilerplate and keeps the three stores consistent from one source of truth. The Annotated[str, Embeddable()/Dedup()] field markers auto-deriving index/identity fields (DataPoint.py:133) is a clean ergonomic.

borrowable.pattern: Deterministic uuid5(name) node IDs for entity merge across chunks/runs without an extra resolution step.
borrowable.why_good: Zero-cost cross-document coreference for exact-name matches and idempotent re-ingestion. Cheap and predictable — a good FAST PATH before any expensive semantic merge.

borrowable.pattern: Per-(type, field) vector collections plus a blended ranking score that fuses cosine distance with importance_weight and feedback_weight.
borrowable.why_good: Lets vector recall seed the graph, then graph structure + learned weights rerank. The feedback EMA update (apply_feedback_weights.py:53) is a simple, defensible online-learning signal to copy.

borrowable.pattern: Vector-seeded k-hop neighborhood projection with re-scoring of expansion nodes (an extra ID-filtered vector search so newly discovered neighbors get real scores).
